coredata.dc:title: Metal-free N-GQDs/P-g-C3N4 photocatalyst with broad-spectrum response: Enhanced exciton dissociation and charge migration for promoting H2 evolution and tetracycline degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yao
Yuan, Jingshu
Ding, Yunji
Zhang, Bolin
Zhang, Shengen
Liu, Bo
coredata.dc:description: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a promising candidate for solving energy and environmental problems. However, narrow photoresponse range, difficult exciton dissociation, and high charge recombination rate severely restrict its activity. Herein, a novel ultrathin porous N-GQDs/P-g-C3N4 (PG-CNS) nanocomposite was fabricated by combining P-element doping, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) coupling, and morphology regulation strategies. The optimal sample (PG-CNS-0.2) was found to extend the photoresponse range over 780 nm due to the combination of midgap state induced by P-doping and excellent upconversion performance of N-GQDs, achieving a fuller utilization of solar energy. Photoluminescence and electrochemical tests confirmed that both exciton dissociation and charge transfer within PG-CNS-0.2 were dramatically enhanced, as reflected by a 3.0-fold increase in carrier density. This enhancement was attributed to reduced exciton binding energy resulting from P-doping, superior electron conduction ability of N-GQDs, as well as larger specific surface area and shorter migration paths provided by the 2D porous structure. Besides, the trapping experimental result indicated that targeted regulation of reactive oxygen species was achieved by changing the addition amount of N-GQDs, thereby enhancing the controllability of photocatalytic reactions. Compared with g-C3N4 nanosheets (CNS), the H2 production rate and tetracycline degradation rate of PG-CNS-0.2 under visible light were improved by 15.7 and 6.9 times, respectively. Meanwhile, the molecular oxygen activation ability of PG-CNS-0.2 was also significantly increased, as reflected by the generation of O2 – and H2O2 at concentrations 2.5 and 3.5 times higher than that of CNS, respectively. This work not only comprehensively reveals the exciton and carrier behaviors in g-C3N4-based reaction systems, but also provides a new strategy for designing low-cost, environmentally friendly, and efficient broad-spectrum responsive photocatalysts.

XPS spectra of the samples: (a) survey spectra (inset: enlarged view of P 2p peaks) and high-resolution of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, and (d) P 2p.
(a) Upconversion spectra of N-GQDs at different excitation wavelengths; (b–e) UV–Vis–NIR DRS (inset: the corresponding bandgap) and (f) VB-XPS spectra of the samples.

(a) PL and (b) time-resolved PL spectra of the samples (τ 1 and τ 2: time constants; τ ave: average lifetime; B 1 and B 2: amplitudes of the components); (c) Photocurrent response of the samples; (d) PH spectra of CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 at various delay times.
Integrated PF intensity as a function of temperature from 77 to 298 K: (a) CNS, (b) P-CNS, and (c) PG-CNS-0.2 (inset: temperature-dependent PF spectra); (d) EIS plots of the samples.
(a) Photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of the samples under visible light; (b) The single-wavelength H2 production activity for CNS, P-CNS, and PG-CNS-0.2.

head.ce:title: Metal-free N-GQDs/P-g-C3N4 photocatalyst with broad-spectrum response: Enhanced exciton dissociation and charge migration for promoting H2 evolution and tetracycline degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a promising candidate for solving energy and environmental problems. However, narrow photoresponse range, difficult exciton dissociation, and high charge recombination rate severely restrict its activity. Herein, a novel ultrathin porous N-GQDs/P-g-C3N4 (PG-CNS) nanocomposite was fabricated by combining P-element doping, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) coupling, and morphology regulation strategies. The optimal sample (PG-CNS-0.2) was found to extend the photoresponse range over 780 nm due to the combination of midgap state induced by P-doping and excellent upconversion performance of N-GQDs, achieving a fuller utilization of solar energy. Photoluminescence and electrochemical tests confirmed that both exciton dissociation and charge transfer within PG-CNS-0.2 were dramatically enhanced, as reflected by a 3.0-fold increase in carrier density. This enhancement was attributed to reduced exciton binding energy resulting from P-doping, superior electron conduction ability of N-GQDs, as well as larger specific surface area and shorter migration paths provided by the 2D porous structure. Besides, the trapping experimental result indicated that targeted regulation of reactive oxygen species was achieved by changing the addition amount of N-GQDs, thereby enhancing the controllability of photocatalytic reactions. Compared with g-C3N4 nanosheets (CNS), the H2 production rate and tetracycline degradation rate of PG-CNS-0.2 under visible light were improved by 15.7 and 6.9 times, respectively. Meanwhile, the molecular oxygen activation ability of PG-CNS-0.2 was also significantly increased, as reflected by the generation of O2 – and H2O2 at concentrations 2.5 and 3.5 times higher than that of CNS, respectively. This work not only comprehensively reveals the exciton and carrier behaviors in g-C3N4-based reaction systems, but also provides a new strategy for designing low-cost, environmentally friendly, and efficient broad-spectrum responsive photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor-based photocatalysis technology is one of the most promising solutions to the global energy crisis and environmental pollution [1,2]. This technology can not only produce clean energy H2 through photocatalytic water splitting but also generate various reactive oxygen species (ROS) via induced molecular oxygen activation, thus mineralizing persistent organic pollutants (POPs) completely [3–6]. Graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a nonmetallic polymer semiconductor material that has attracted much attention because of its low cost, easy preparation, suitable bandgap (E g ≈ 2.7 eV), and high stability. However, its photocatalytic efficiency has remained severely restricted by the following factors. (1) Pristine g-C3N4 can only respond to visible light at wavelengths below 450 nm, which results in its insensitivity to broad visible light and near-infrared light (NIR, ∼ 53 % of the full spectrum) with lower energy and longer wavelengths, thus severely limiting its use of solar energy. (2) As a polymer semiconductor, g-C3N4 has been demonstrated to have a huge exciton effect [7,8]. High exciton binding energy (E b) leads to difficult exciton dissociation, which is an important restrictive factor for the photocatalytic efficiency of g-C3N4. (3) The interlayer π-electron conjugation effect and small specific surface area (S BET, ∼ 10 m2g−1) of bulk g-C3N4 result in severe charge recombination and insufficient surface active sites, thus restricting the photocatalytic performance [9–11]. Therefore, it is urgent to seek a strategy that can simultaneously address the aforementioned issues.

The small S BET and complex migratory route of bulk g-C3N4 lead to easy recombination of successfully dissociated excitons, which is also one of the reasons underlying the high charge recombination rate [10,12]. Considering that excitonic effects stem from Coulomb interactions, while dielectric shielding within low-dimensional semiconductors exhibits significant size and/or thickness dependence [13,14]. It is therefore hypothesized that constructing 2D ultrathin porous structures can provide shorter carrier transport routes and more active sites, thus facilitating exciton dissociation and charge migration [15–17]. Nevertheless, a single modification method cannot optimize the performance of g-C3N4 to the maximum, and coupling it with other materials is an effective strategy.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are single-layer or few-layer graphene sheets with a few nanometers in size [3]. Because of their unique edge effect, quantum effect, and excellent electrical conductivity, GQDs have attracted widespread attention [18]. In addition, it is shown that N-doping can further enhance the optical and electron transport property of GQDs [19,20]. On the one hand, N-GQDs possess superior upconversion photoluminescence (PL) properties that allow them to act as a photosensitizer to capture visible and NIR light. On the other hand, they also have outstanding electron conduction ability and can serve as electron sinks and reservoirs to facilitate charge dissociation and migration [21]. Therefore, coupling N-GQDs could be a favorable choice to extend the excellent performance of g-C3N4 to the red limit of visible light while enhancing exciton dissociation and charge separation. Unfortunately, the photocatalytic activity of the composites obtained by simply coupling N-GQDs with g-C3N4 is still unsatisfactory. The reason for this is that most upconversion light wavelengths for N-GQDs are around 500 nm, and pristine g-C3N4 cannot entirely exploit these wavelengths (λ less than 450 nm) [21]. To solve the above problem, the g-C3N4 bandgap needs to be narrowed first to extend its response range to visible light. By exploiting the localized surface plasmon resonance effect of Ag nanoparticles, Deng et al. made some improvements to the issue that g-C3N4 cannot adequately use the upconverted light by N-GQDs [21]. Yet, considering the high cost of Ag, it is not suitable for further research. Doping with nonmetallic elements is known to be a good choice for narrowing the bandgap, however, most elements such as N, F, and O can only slightly change the bandgap (less than 0.2 eV generally) [22–24]. Whereas, P-doping can significantly broaden the photoabsorption range of the material by forming a midgap state [25–27]. For example, Zhang et al [28] modified bulk g-C3N4 with P-doping and NCDs, and found that the resulting composites had enhanced H2 evolution activity. But unfortunately, this study ignored the strong exciton effect in g-C3N4 and only modulated and explained the change in properties from a single carrier perspective. Notably, P-doping has been shown to effectively reduce the E b within g-C3N4, thereby inducing more excitons to dissociate more readily [29]. Therefore, it is speculated that co-modification of ultrathin porous g-C3N4 nanosheets by P element and N-GQDs is expected to simultaneously achieve full visible and even NIR photoresponse, as well as exciton dissociation and carrier migration enhancement. There have not been reported studies combining midgap state induced by P-doping, upconversion effect of N-GQDs, and morphology modulation to our knowledge.

In this study, the novel ultrathin porous g-C3N4 nanosheets co-modified by P element and N-GQDs (i.e., PG-CNS-0.2 sample) was designed and fabricated. The effect and mechanism of P-doping and coupling N-GQDs on the photoabsorption ability of the samples were investigated by DRS, ESR, and performance tests. The mechanism of P-doping, coupling N-GQDs, and morphology modulation on the conversion behaviors among singlet excitons, triplet excitons, and carriers within PG-CNS-0.2 were explored by PL (steady-state/transient fluorescence, steady-state phosphorescence, temperature-dependent fluorescence) and electrochemical tests. With the synergistic effect of the three modifications as aforementioned, the photoabsorption ability, exciton dissociation as well as charge transport ability of PG-CNS-0.2 were enhanced. Compared with pristine g-C3N4 nanosheets, PG-CNS-0.2 displayed superior photocatalytic H2 evolution and organic pollutant removal, which was closely associated with a notable improvement in ROS generation. related to the significant improvement of ROS generation. Furthermore, this work also investigated the effect and mechanism of the addition amount of N-GQDs on ROS generation species. Finally, a reasonable mechanism for the performance improvement of PG-CNS-0.2 was proposed.
The analytical-grade reagents listed in the Supplementary Information (SI) were all employed with no further purification (Text S1).

N-GQDs were prepared by a slightly modified hydrothermal method [30]. Specifically, 1.2188 g ammonium citrate was dissolved into 10 mL deionized water, followed by 7 drops of ethylenediamine drop by drop. The mixture solution was stirred and well-mixed and then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave, which was treated at 200 °C for 6 h. The resulting tan color N-GQDs stock solution was filtered with 0.22 μm filters and dialyzed (3500 Da cutoff) for 24 h and then dried at 75 °C under vacuum. Finally, solid N-GQDs were obtained.

According to the pre-experimental results (Table S1 and Fig. S1), the resulting P-doped g-C3N4 nanosheets (denoted as P-CNS in this study) possess the best photocatalytic degradation performance when the mass ratio of melamine (ME) to 2-aminoethylphosphonic acid (AEP) is 40:0.8. The actual content of P element in the P-CNS sample is 0.99 wt%, which was tested by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [31]. Therefore, this raw material mass ratio was chosen to prepare the P-CNS sample in this study. In particular, ME and AEP were mixed in 60 mL deionized water at the mass ratio as aforementioned and then stirred at 75 °C to complete the evaporation of water. The resulting white solid was a mixture of ME and AEP. 1 g mixture was mixed with specific amounts of N-GQDs (0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3 wt%) in 70 mL deionized water, stirred magnetically for 24 h and then dried at 75 °C. Subsequently, the obtained light brown solid was grinded and transferred to a ceramic boat with cover, heated to 500 °C under N2 ambiance, and held for 3 h, followed by heating to 550 °C and held for 5 h. The heating rate was set at 4 °C/min. After natural cooling, the resulting solid was grinded and transferred to an uncovered crucible, further heated to 550 °C under an air atmosphere, and held for 5 h. The obtained gray-brown powders after cooling were N-GQDs/P-CNS (PG-CNS) nanocomposites. The composites were labeled as PG-CNS-x (x = 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3) according to the different doping ratios of N-GQDs. Repeating the above operations, P-CNS was obtained without the addition of N-GQDs to the raw material; N-GQDs-modified g-C3N4 nanosheets (G-CNS) were produced by adding 0.2 wt% N-GQDs without the addition of AEP; Ultrathin porous g-C3N4 nanosheets (CNS) were prepared without the addition of N-GQDs and AEP. The raw material ratio and the actual content of P and N elements for as-prepared samples are listed in Table S2.
Detailed descriptions of characterization methods and photocatalytic performance tests are provided in the SI (Text S2–S5).

Analyzing the crystal structures of different samples was conducted through X-ray diffraction (XRD, Fig. 1 a). All samples show canonical peak features of g-C3N4 at around 13° and 27°, corresponding to (100) and (002) crystal planes, respectively [2]. The presence of two characteristic peaks represents the typical graphite-like layered stacking structure in g-C3N4 [25,32,33]. Because of the high dispersion and low content of N-GQDs, it is difficult to observe its characteristic peaks in the XRD patterns of G-CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 samples [34,35]. Likewise, no P-element-related diffraction peaks are observed as a result of the low P content. Additionally, the peak shape of the (002) crystal plane in the samples coupled with N-GQDs, especially for PG-CNS-0.2, tends to broaden and weaken compared to CNS, with a slight shift to a small angle (inset in Fig. 1a) [35–37]. This variation could be explained by the presence of N-GQDs not only on the surface of g-C3N4 but also between its interlayers, thereby influencing the layer spacing of g-C3N4 [35,38]. All samples display similar XRD patterns, indicating that thermal exfoliation, P-doping, and coupling N-GQDs did not change the original crystal structure of g-C3N4.

The molecular structure information of the samples before and after modification was analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 1b. All samples exhibit similar FT-IR spectra. Specifically, the broad vibration bands at 3000–3500 cm−1 can be ascribed to the COH and NH stretching vibrations [2,39]. The stretching vibration modes in the range of 1200–1700 cm−1 and s-triazine ring mode at 810 cm−1 are credited to CN and CN heterocycles [34]. Notably, the intensity of the absorption peaks was found to be reduced for the samples after coupling N-GQDs, especially for PG-CNS-0.2, compared to CNS. This change may be attributed to the more disordered arrangement of hydrogen-bonded long chains of polymeric melon units in PG-CNS-0.2 nanosheets after N-GQDs coupling [35]. And the deteriorated orderliness may be related to the widened g-C3N4 layer spacing caused by coupled N-GQDs [35]. Besides, it is difficult to detect the characteristic peaks associated with N-GQDs and P because of the low loading and strong stretching vibrations of the CN bond [25,34]. The similar spectra of CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 imply that the doping of P element and N-GQDs did not destroy the surface chemical structure of g-C3N4 [37]. This result is essential to improve the photocatalytic activity and is also in accordance with the XRD results.

Field emission transmission electron microscopy (FETEM) was employed to characterize the morphological structure and particle size of the as-prepared photocatalysts. As seen in Fig. 2 a, the prepared N-GQDs are dispersed sphere-like with a lattice spacing of 0.20 nm, corresponding to the (101) crystal plane of graphite [40,41]. Their sizes are mainly distributed from 2.6 nm to 6.7 nm, with an average diameter of approximately 4.5 nm (Fig. 2b), indicating that N-GQDs have been successfully prepared. From Fig. 2c, it can be seen that PG-CNS-0.2 shows an ultrathin porous structure. To further obtain the real thickness of PG-CNS-0.2 sample, atomic force microscopy (AFM) test was also performed. The AFM image and the corresponding height histograms of PG-CNS-0.2 are presented in Fig. 2d and e. The cross-sectional AFM images show that the thickness of PG-CNS-0.2 is determined to be 2.7–2.9 nm, which is comparable to or thinner than previous reports [4,12,42–44], strongly confirming that PG-CNS-0.2 has an ultrathin nanostructure. Furthermore, from Fig. S2 and Fig. 2c, PG-CNS-0.2 obviously has a more abundant pore structure than CNS, which is associated with the release of large amounts of ammonia during high-temperature polymerization of AEP and ME. The open-pore structure on the surface of these materials can increase their S BET and provide more active sites for reactions, as well as facilitate the separation and migration of carriers. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 2f and the inset, many black dots are clearly and uniformly distributed on the surface of PG-CNS-0.2. By further observation of its high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2g), it is found that the lattice spacing of the black dots is 0.20 nm, indicating that N-GQDs have been successfully loaded on g-C3N4 [37]. Finally, the results of elemental mapping analysis indicate (Fig. 2h–l) that PG-CNS-0.2 contains three elements, such as C, N, and P. These elements are uniformly dispersed in PG-CNS-0.2 matrix, further confirming that PG-CNS-0.2 was successfully prepared. To provide more evidence, the C/N ratios of different samples were also investigated by quantitative X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis and elemental analyzer (EA) (see Text S6, Table S3, and Table S4 for detailed analysis and results), and the results further confirmed that PG-CNS-0.2 has been successfully obtained.

The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of CNS, G-CNS, P-CNS, and PG-CNS-0.2 are shown in Fig. 3 and the inset, and the corresponding S BET and pore volumes are listed in Table S5. The isotherm types of all samples are type-IV with H3 hysteresis loops, suggesting the presence of mesoporous structures on the material surface [45,46]. In comparison with CNS (36.0 m2g−1, 0.10 cm3g−1), the S BET of P-CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 increases to 53.2 m2g−1 and 52.2 m2g−1, and the corresponding pore volume increases to 0.14 cm3g−1 and 0.12 cm3g−1, respectively. This increment is attributed to the release of large amounts of ammonia from both ME and AEP during the thermal polycondensation process. Besides, in contrast to CNS and P-CNS, their corresponding samples after coupling N-GQDs (i.e., G-CNS, and PG-CNS-0.2) have slightly reduced S BET and pore volume. This reduction could be due to the coupled N-GQDs occupying part of the pores of the ultrathin porous g-C3N4 nanosheets [35,47]. As is known, larger S BET can supply more active sites and facilitate the adsorption of pollutant molecules, while proper pore structure can shorten the distance of carrier migration to the reaction sites, thus improving the photocatalytic performance.

The chemical composition of the synthesized N-GQDs was investigated by XPS. As shown in Fig. S3a, the XPS spectrum of N-GQDs reveals three main peaks [3,41,48], corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s in Fig. S3b includes three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV, and 287.7 eV, which correspond to the CC/CC bond [49–51], CO bond [52,53], and CO bond [54,55], respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s in Fig. S3c can be deconvoluted into three peaks: CNC (399.0 eV) [56], CN (399.9 eV) [57], and NH groups (400.7 eV) [58,59]. The above analysis confirms that N atoms have been successfully introduced into GQDs.

Additionally, XPS analysis was also used to further investigate the surface composition and chemical state of the samples. As shown in XPS survey spectra of CNS, P-CNS, and PG-CNS-0.2 (Fig. 4 a), the samples clearly contain C, N, and O. Moreover, a small amount of P element is present in the XPS spectra of P-CNS and PG-CNS-0.2, which indicates the successful incorporation of P element. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra of all samples (Fig. 4b and Fig. S4a) contain three peaks at 284.8 eV, ∼ 286.1 eV, and ∼ 288.4 eV, corresponding to graphite CC bond [60], CO bond [21,30], and sp2-hybridized carbon (NCN) [12], respectively [61]. In the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 4c and Fig. S4b), all samples can be deconvoluted into four peaks at ∼ 398.5 eV (CNC) [30], ∼399.8 eV (NC3 bond) [1,44], 400.9 eV (amino functional group CNH) [62–64], and ∼ 404.4 eV (charging effect) [40,63]. Notably, the peaks of P-CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 are slightly shifted compared to CNS, which could be explained by the introduction of P elements and N-GQDs [62]. Specifically, the binding energies of both C 1 s and N 1 s peaks are slightly changed in P-CNS compared to CNS, indicating that P-doping can modulate the electronic properties and chemical environment of the composites [65–67]. Since P has a lower electronegativity (2.544 for C and 2.253 for P) and also has more outer valence electrons than C when part of the C atom is replaced by P, it will be easier for the N atom to acquire more charge than before (i.e., an increase in electron density) [29,68,69]. Further, when the electrons in the P atom flow to the N atom, the charge density difference between the two gradually increases and the electrons undergo a polarization distribution, which leads to a change in the electron density of C as well [69,70]. Compared to P-CNS, the change in C 1 s and N 1 s binding energy for PG-CNS-0.2 is probably due to the high electron conduction ability of the introduced N-GQDs [21], leading to a decrease in charge density around P-CNS [71,72]. The high-resolution P 2p spectra (Fig. 4d) show a peak at 133.1 eV, corresponding to the PN bond [73,74]. Besides, no PC bond was found in the P 2p spectrum, suggesting that P forms a PN bond by replacing C in the CN framework, which is consistent with the existing studies [1,60,75]. The above results indicate that even after P element and N-GQDs were incorporated into g-C3N4 successfully, the surface chemical structure of g-C3N4 was not adversely affected.

To confirm the upconversion performance of the N-GQDs sample, its upconversion spectra at different excitation wavelengths were investigated. The emission wavelengths of N-GQDs basically remain in the range of 450–550 nm when excited by 500–1000 nm wavelengths (Fig. 5 a). This feature suggests that the prepared N-GQDs can absorb long wavelengths of light and convert them to short wavelengths (450–550 nm) of light. Thus, combining the excellent upconversion performance of N-GQDs can expand the photoresponse range of the material, thereby contributing to the generation of more photogenerated e––h+ pairs to enhance photocatalytic activity.

The optical characteristics of the samples were investigated by ultraviolet–visible–near–infrared diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis–NIR DRS). As Fig. 5b shows, the optical absorption edge of CNS at around 450 nm. When coupling N-GQDs, the optical absorption edge of the G-CNS sample does not change significantly (Fig. 5c). However, the photoabsorption of G-CNS is slightly enhanced throughout the visible and even near-infrared range due to the strong visible absorption and excellent upconversion of N-GQDs. In addition, the optical absorption edge of P-CNS is red-shifted to about 500 nm compared to CNS (Fig. 5d), indicating a change in the bandgap. Compared with the samples aforementioned, the photoabsorption of PG-CNS-0.2 is dramatically increased in the whole visible and NIR regions (Fig. 5e), owing to the synergistic effect between N-GQDs and P-element co-doping. To further study the reasons for the variation of the photoabsorption ability, we determined the specific energy band positions of the samples via combining the Kubelk–Munk method (inset of Fig. 5b–e) and valence-band XPS (VB-XPS) spectra (Fig. 5f), and the results are summarized in Table S6. Notably, both P-CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 exhibit strong absorption tails (Urbach tail) between 400 and 1000 nm (Fig. 5d–e), which is in accordance with existing studies [60]. The appearance of this absorption tail results from the midgap state induced by P-doping, which can drive g-C3N4 to utilize photons with energy smaller than its bandgap energy.

To determine how P-doping-induced midgap states influence the photoabsorption ability of the materials, Kubelk–Munk method was adopted to calculate the transition energies (E t) from VB to the midgap state in P-CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 as 2.21 eV and 2.17 eV, respectively [60,75]. Then, the exact positions of the corresponding midgap state were calculated both to be –0.06 eV and –0.06 eV, respectively. This result implies that the presence of the midgap state would extend the optical absorption edge of P-CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 to 561 and 571 nm, respectively. Remarkably, compared with P-CNS, PG-CNS-0.2 shows further enhancement of photoabsorption in the entire visible and NIR regions, mainly resulting from the synergy of N-GQDs and P element (Fig. 5d and e). Specifically, the upconversion characteristics of N-GQDs can convert NIR light to visible light around 450–550 nm, which can still not be fully utilized by CNS. Nevertheless, the midgap state induced by P-doping can extend the photoabsorption range to about 560 nm. This expansion allows g-C3N4 to take full advantage of exactly the light converted on N-GQDs, which in turn leads to a significant improvement in photoabsorption of PG-CNS-0.2 in the entire visible and NIR region.

It is crucial for improving photocatalytic performance to achieve adequate exciton dissociation and carrier migration. Therefore, the population of singlet (S1) excitons was researched by steady-state PL spectroscopy (Fig. 6 a). With respect to CNS, which has the highest peak intensity, the emissions peak intensities for P-CNS and G-CNS are much lower, with the lowest peak intensity being achieved for PG-CNS-0.2. Given that fluorescence stems from radiative decay of S1 excitons, the dramatic reduction in the intensity of emission peak represents a significantly reduced population of S1 excitons [12,76,77]. Usually, there are two factors involved in the decrease in S1 excitons [78]. (1) S1 excitons dissociate into free charges; (2) S1 excitons transform into triplet (T1) excitons via intersystem crossing (ISC) process. Verifying that the decrease in S1 excitons in PG-CNS-0.2 results from the first factor, we conducted the following verification from the number of free charges, the number of T1 excitons, and the change of exciton binding energy (E b), respectively.

Firstly, we studied the variation of the number of free charges in the samples. (1) Time-resolved PL spectra of CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 were monitored at the corresponding steady-state emission peaks. Also, a biexponential model was used to fit their fluorescence decay curves (Fig. 6b, Eqn. S1). The average lifetimes (τ ave) of S1 excitons in CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 were calculated to be 17.94 ns and 7.18 ns, respectively. The considerably shortened lifetime clearly indicates the enhancement of S1 excitons dissociation in PG-CNS-0.2 [12,76,78]. (2) The results of transient photocurrent tests yielded consistent conclusions as aforementioned. As shown in Fig. 6c, the order of photocurrent signal strength for all samples is CNS < G-CNS < P-CNS < PG-CNS-0.2. The strengthened photocurrent signal implies more free charge production and better e––h+ pairs separation efficiency [3]. (3) The charge carrier density (N D) was quantified by Mott–Schottky (M−S) test (Fig. S5). The slopes of curves are all positive, representing the N-type behavior for all samples. The N D of all samples was calculated from Eqn. S2 [79] and listed in Table S6. PG-CNS-0.2 still has the largest N D (2.84 × 1022 cm−3), which is 1.5, 1.7, and 3.0 times larger than P-CNS, G-CNS, and CNS, respectively. Based on the analysis aforementioned, the number of free charges in PG-CNS-0.2 substantially increased compared with that in other samples. Accordingly, it is reasonable to attribute the decrease in S1 excitons in PG-CNS-0.2 to their dissociation into free charges.

Secondly, for providing an additional demonstration, it is also verified in reverse by observing the behaviors of T1 excitons via delayed fluorescence experiments. Different from fluorescence (PF), the phosphorescence (PH) signal arises from the radiation decay of T1 excitons, thus reflecting the change in the population of T1 excitons. The PF shows a shorter lifetime than the PH because of the spin-flip limitation, allowing the two to be differentiated with a specific delay period [7,78,80]. Fig. 6d displays the PH spectra of CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 at various delay times. It is found that the PH intensity of PG-CNS-0.2 is always obviously lower than that of CNS with the change of delay time. Furthermore, they both decrease gradually while maintaining little change in relative intensity. This result shows that T1 concentration in PG-CNS-0.2 decreased rather than increased compared to CNS, i.e., S1 excitons did not convert into T1 excitons. As for the lower initial T1 concentration in PG-CNS-0.2, it could be originated from the lower S1 excitons yield [76]. To further confirm that S1 excitons did not transform into T1 excitons, the energy difference between PF and PH emission peaks was also monitored (Fig. S6 a and b). The resulting S1–T1 energy gaps (ΔE ST) for CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 were estimated to be 0.131 eV and 0.241 eV, respectively. The increased ΔE ST means that the conversion from S1 into T1 becomes more difficult in PG-CNS-0.2, and both the ISC efficiency and the T1 excitons yield are reduced. This result again indicates that the decline in S1 excitons is not due to their transformation into T1 excitons. In conclusion, it can be determined from the above forward and reverse verifications that the decrease in S1 excitons in PG-CNS-0.2 results from the fact that S1 dissociated into free charges instead of converting into T1.

Finally, to provide more direct evidence, the E b of the samples was also investigated by temperature-dependent PF spectroscopy (Fig. 7 a–c). The intensity of integrated PF peaks for CNS, P-CNS, and PG-CNS-0.2 all decreases with the increase in temperature. By fitting the data obtained in the experiment through the Arrhenius equation: I(T) = I 0/(1 + Aexp(–E b/k B T), the corresponding E b was obtained. Where I 0 is the peak intensity at 0 K, A is constant, E b is exciton binding energy, and k B is Boltzmann constant. The calculation results show that the E b of P-CNS is reduced to 67 meV compared to CNS (80 meV), confirming that P-doping can effectively lower the E b of g-C3N4 and thus induce more excitons dissociation into free charges. This result is in accordance with our previous study [29]. Notably, the E b of PG-CNS-0.2 (74 meV) is slightly larger than that of P-CNS, but still significantly lower than that of CNS. This indicates on the one hand that coupling N-GQDs is not the main reason for E b reduction in PG-CNS-0.2, and on the other hand that more excitons will dissociate into free charges in PG-CNS-0.2 than in CNS. The above analysis shows that P-doping is the dominant cause of E b decrease in PG-CNS-0.2, while N-GQDs with excellent electron transfer capability mainly provide fast separation channels for the already dissociated excitons. With P-doping and coupling N-GQDs, PG-CNS-0.2 exhibits dramatically enhanced exciton dissociation and carrier migration efficiency.

Furthermore, the transfer behaviors of interfacial charges were investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (Fig. 7d). Among all samples, CNS presents the largest arc radius, followed by G-CNS and P-CNS, PG-CNS-0.2 has the smallest arc radius. The smaller arc radius means a lower charge transfer resistance [81]. It is evident that PG-CNS-0.2 has not only the highest exciton dissociation and charge separation efficiency but also the fastest electron migration rate among the four samples.

The photocatalytic H2 generation activity of the samples was evaluated under visible light (λ > 420 nm). As shown in Fig. 8 a, CNS (152 μmol h−1 g−1) has the lowest H2 production rate, which is related to its large internal exciton effect and charge recombination rate. When introducing P element and N-GQDs, the H2 production rates of P-CNS and G-CNS are found to be enhanced to 1725 μmol h−1 g−1 and 1584 μmol h−1 g−1, respectively. Notably, the H2 production properties of PG-CNS-x systems are dramatically enhanced by the synergy of P-doping and coupling N-GQDs. When the N-GQDs content is 0.2 wt%, the sample (PG-CN-0.2) reaches the highest H2 production rate (2386 μmol h−1 g−1), which is 1.4, 1.5, and 15.7 times higher than that of P-CNS, G-CNS, and CNS under the same conditions, respectively. Remarkably, the photocatalytic H2 evolution of PG-CN-0.2 is comparable to or even higher than the results of similar photocatalysts reported previously (Table S7). However, when the addition of N-GQDs is further increased, the performance of PG-CNS-x composites starts to decrease. This change indicates that N-GQDs content plays a significant role in the photocatalytic activity of the composites. In addition, the changing rule of H2 production rate for PG-CNS-x photocatalysts is found to be consistent with the corresponding PL spectra. It is suggested that for PG-CNS-x systems, exciton dissociation rate and carrier mobility could be crucial factors affecting the photocatalytic reaction.

To confirm the broadened photoabsorption and enhanced photoactivity of PG-CNS-0.2, we performed single-wavelength photocatalytic H2 production tests on CNS, P-CNS, and PG-CNS-0.2 (Fig. 8b). Apparently, CNS can show H2 production activity up to a single wavelength light source of 450 nm. Benefiting from the midgap state induced by P-doping, the edge of visible light response for P-CNS is extended to about 560 nm. And, P-CNS can still exhibit low activity up to a single-wavelength light source of 550 nm. Remarkably, under the synergy of P-doping and coupling N-GQDs, PG-CNS-0.2 can still display good H2 production activity at a single wavelength of 700 nm (9.7 μmol h−1 g−1). Besides, the H2 production stability of PG-CNS-0.2 was investigated by 4 cycling tests. As seen in Fig. S7, no significant variation is observed in the recovered samples, indicating that PG-CNS-0.2 has better stability.

The photocatalytic ability of the samples was assessed through degrading tetracycline (TC) under visible light (λ > 420 nm). The photocatalytic process was preceded by a 60 min dark reaction to reach adsorption–desorption balance. Besides, the photodegradation test was performed without adding a photocatalyst to exclude the degradation of TC by photolysis. As shown in Fig. 9 a, a series of gradient tests were conducted to determine the optimized loading of N-GQDs. After 60 min of irradiation, CNS photodegrades 24.7 % of TC. When introducing P-element or N-GQDs, the degradation efficiency of TC by P-CNS and G-CNS increases to 65.2 % and 57.3 %, respectively, which is still not satisfactory. Significantly, the optical absorption edge of g-C3N4 is about 450 nm, while the upconversion effect of N-GQDs can only convert long-wavelength light to visible light in the range of 450–550 nm. Thus, g-C3N4 is incapable of effectively utilizing the vast majority of upconverted light. To further improve the light utilization, it is an excellent choice to narrow the bandgap by P-doping to expand the g-C3N4 photoresponse range and thus make full use of the light obtained by upconversion. It is shown that the photodegradation performance of PG-CNS-x systems are obviously enhanced by the synergy of P-doping and coupling N-GQDs. Similar to H2 production results, the degradation property of PG-CNS-x tends to increase first with increasing N-GQDs and reach the optimum at the dosage of 0.2 wt% (PG-CNS-0.2, 84.7 %), which is 1.3, 1.5, and 3.4 times higher than that of P-CNS, G-CNS, and CNS, respectively. However, when N-GQDs doping is further increased, the performance of PG-CNS-x starts to decrease dramatically since excess N-GQDs would be the carrier recombination center. To visualize the differences in photocatalytic performance of the samples, their photodegradation kinetics were also investigated, and the reaction process was found to follow the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Fig. 9b). The corresponding apparent rate constant (k) can be calculated by the equation (k = ln (C0 /Ct )/t), and the trend corresponds to the degradation activity. The k of PG-CNS-0.2 is 0.0310 min−1, which is 1.9, 2.2, and 6.9 times higher than that of P-CNS, G-CNS, and CNS, respectively. The variation pattern of degradation performance for PG-CNS-x systems is consistent with the corresponding PL spectral result, which likewise indicates that exciton dissociation and carrier mobility efficiency contribute substantially to the photocatalytic property of PG-CNS-x.

To confirm the enhanced photoresponsiveness of PG-CNS-0.2, we further investigated the photocatalytic degradation property of TC under full spectral conditions (λ > 365 nm) (Fig. 9c and d). Compared with visible light conditions, all samples show increased photodegradation efficiency under full spectral conditions. The TC degradation efficiency for the optimal sample (PG-CNS-0.2) reaches 89.9 % within 60 min, and the corresponding k is 0.0373 min−1. This is because the short wavelengths of light in the full spectrum can provide higher energy, thereby exciting the material to generate more photogenerated carriers to promote photocatalytic performance. Additionally, the ability of the samples to photodegrade TC under NIR condition (λ > 780 nm) was also evaluated separately, and the results are shown in Fig. 9e and f. Due to the longer wavelength and weaker energy of NIR light under which all samples show significantly reduced photocatalytic activity than under visible or full-spectrum light irradiation. Notably, PG-CNS-0.2 still presents the best photocatalytic performance compared with other samples. After irradiating under NIR light for 60 min, PG-CNS-0.2 can still degrade 26.4 % of TC with a corresponding k of 0.0046 min−1, which owes to the synergy of P-element and N-GQDs. Also, the degradation activity of PG-CNS-0.2 against rhodamine B and ciprofloxacin was investigated as shown in Fig. S8. The results show that PG-CNS-0.2 has promising generalizability.

The stability of the composites was assessed by cycling tests (Fig. S9a), as described in the experimental section. PG-CNS-0.2 degradation efficiency is not significantly decreased after 4 cycling tests, which proves its excellent performance stability. Moreover, comparing the XRD and FTIR results of PG-CNS-0.2 before and after use (Fig. S9b and c), no significant changes are found, confirming that PG-CNS-0.2 has an excellent phase and molecular structure stability. In addition, the TOC removal efficiency of the samples during TC degradation was also investigated. As in Fig. S9d, the TOC removal rate of PG-CNS-0.2 (32.8 %) is significantly higher than that of CNS (11.5 %) after 60 min irradiation. This result indicates that P-CNS-0.2 not only shows better TC degradation efficiency but also exhibits higher mineralization ability.

To determine the contribution of different active species to TC photocatalytic degradation, a radical trapping experiment was conducted by adding the corresponding trapping agent (Fig. 10 a and b). The p-benzoquinone (p-BQ), isopropanol (IPA), furfuryl alcohol (FA), and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na) were chosen as the trapping agents for O2 –, OH, 1O2, and h+, respectively. It is found that the presence of IPA (21.6 %) and p-BQ (16.4 %) does not significantly affect the photodegradation efficiency of CNS. However, the degradation efficiency of CNS dramatically decreases from 24.7 % to 12.2 % and 7.3 % when adding EDTA-2Na and FA, respectively. This reduction suggests that 1O2 plays a dominant role in TC degradation by CNS, followed by h+. However, when adding IPA, FA, EDTA-2Na, and p-BQ alone, the degradation efficiency of PG-CNS-0.2 on TC fall from 84.7 % to 72.6 %, 61.2 %, 40.3 %, and 28.6 %, respectively. This result indicates that O2 – acts the primary role in TC degradation by PG-CNS-0.2, followed by h+, and 1O2 and OH contribute relatively small.

Given the key role of O2 – in photocatalysis, we quantified the O2 – generated during the reaction by the nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) conversion experiment. The absorbance at 259 nm for all samples diminishes monotonically with time, which owes to the continuous O2 – production (Fig. S10). As shown in Fig. 10c and d, PG-CNS-0.2 exhibits the best NBT conversion efficiency (29.7 %) after 60 min irradiation. The corresponding k = 0.0052 min−1 is 1.7, 1.9, and 2.7 times higher than that of P-CNS, G-CNS, and CNS, respectively, resulting from the increased O2 – production. Further, we calculated the O2 – steady-state concentration ([O2 –]ss) from Eqn. S3 [4]. The inset of Fig. 10c shows that PG-CNS-0.2 has the highest [O2 –]ss of 12.10 × 10–10 M, which exceeds that of P-CNS, G-CNS, and CNS by 1.6, 1.7, and 2.5 times, respectively. This result reveals that PG-CNS-0.2 possesses a distinctly increased molecular oxygen activation capacity. Besides, the above conclusions were verified by monitoring the amount of H2O2 production. Based on the relationship between H2O2 and I3 –, it is concluded that H2O2 production on PG-CNS-0.2 is still the highest among the four samples, which is 1.6, 2.1, and 3.5 times higher for P-CNS, G-CNS, and CNS, respectively (Fig. S11a). The enhanced H2O2 generation ability further confirms that the synergy of P-doping and coupling N-GQDs favors the improved molecular oxygen activation capacity of g-C3N4. Finally, to provide additional evidence, the electron spin resonance (ESR) test for O2 – was also performed on CNS, P-CNS, G-CNS, and PG-CNS-0.2 (see Text S7 and Fig. S11b). The ESR results were found to be consistent with the above conclusion.

To verify the various ROS production, ESR tests were conducted under dark and visible light, respectively (Fig. 11 ). No characteristic peaks are observed for both CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 in the dark. Nevertheless, the DMPO–O2 – signal peaks are recognized under visible light for both samples, which correspond to an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1. PG-CNS-0.2 has a distinctly stronger peak intensity than CNS, which means that PG-CNS-0.2 is capable of producing more O2 – (Fig. 11a). In addition, the signal peaks of DMPO–OH and TEMPO–1O2 are also detected in both systems, and the signal peak of PG-CNS-0.2 is stronger than that of CNS (Fig. 11b and c). The above results show that both CNS and PG-CNS-0.2 can generate O2 –, OH, and 1O2, yet PG-CNS-0.2 can generate more. The results again confirm that P-doping and coupling N-GQDs can dramatically improve the molecular oxygen activation of g-C3N4, which corresponds to the prominent photocatalytic activity of PG-CNS-0.2. Furthermore, the ESR spectra of the samples under NIR condition (λ > 780 nm) were also tested individually for a more comprehensive analysis of generated radical species (Fig. 11d–f). The characteristic peaks of O2 –, OH, and 1O2 are found to be still detectable in PG-CNS-0.2 system, however, they are hardly observable in the CNS system, which agrees with the photocatalytic degradation results of both.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Targeted regulation of ROS by N-GQDs addition and the mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: For studying the effect of the addition amount of N-GQDs on ROS and the mechanism, the representative PG-CNS-0.1, PG-CNS-0.2, and PG-CNS-0.3 samples were selected for the radical trapping test, and the results are shown in Fig. 12 . It turns out that when the doping of N-GQDs is less than or equal to 0.2 wt%, the ROS that acts as the primary effect during the reaction is O2 –. And when the doping of N-GQDs > 0.2 wt%, the main ROS becomes 1O2. The variation of the main ROS is closely related to the degree of exciton dissociation and charge separation within the material [77]. Specifically, O2 – is formed by the reaction between free electrons and O2, and its production is directly correlated with the number of free electrons (Eqn. (7)). Whereas, 1O2 can be generated both by the energy transfer process between T1 and O2 (Eqn. (5)) and by the charge transfer process between h+ and O2 – (Eqn. (8)). From the PL result (Fig. 6a), PG-CNS-0.2 has the lowest PL intensity, indicating its highest efficiency of internal exciton dissociation and charge separation, which helps to generate more photogenerated electrons for O2 – generation. Compared with PG-CNS-0.2, the PL intensity of PG-CNS-0.3 is significantly enhanced, even higher than that of PG-CNS-0.1. This enhancement represents a dramatic decrease in exciton dissociation and charge separation efficiency within PG-CNS-0.3, resulting in a significant reduction of holes, as well as of the photogenerated electrons used to generate O2 –. Hence, PG-CNS-0.3 prefers to generate 1O2 via an energy transfer process (Eqn. (5)). The result of photocurrent response for three samples also further confirms the conclusion as aforementioned (Fig. S12a). Notably, the degree of exciton dissociation and carrier separation is also dependent on the doping amount of N-GQDs. According to Fig. 9a, the performance of PG-CNS-x first gradually increases with the increased doping of N-GQDs and reaches the highest at 0.2 wt%. This improvement results from the excellent electron conduction ability of N-GQDs, which could contribute efficiently to exciton dissociation and carrier transport, thereby providing more free electrons for O2 – production. However, too many N-GQDs would instead become charge recombination centers [21,82], covering the active site of PG-CNS-x and weakening its own electron transfer. In this case, the free charge inside the material is drastically reduced, resulting in a large reduction of free electrons for O2 – generation, thus preferring to generate 1O2 via energy transfer. Therefore, it is speculated that the degree of exciton dissociation and charge separation within the material can be changed by adjusting the doping amount of N-GQDs, thereby performing a targeted regulation of the active species. To further verify the above speculation, ESR tests were also conducted for PG-CNS-0.1, PG-CNS-0.2, and PG-CNS-0.3 (see Text S8 and Fig. S12b–d for details). The results suggest that the three samples can all produce O2 –, 1O2, and OH, but PG-CNS-0.2 produces more, which is consistent with the results of the radical trapping experiments. The ESR results again confirm that the regulation of active species can be targeted by adjusting the doping amount of N-GQDs.

Surprisingly, both O2 – and 1O2 are strongly oxidizing, yet why the photocatalytic performance of PG-CNS-0.3 is obviously weaker than that of PG-CN-0.2? This is because, for PG-CNS-0.2, the enhanced exciton dissociation allows the generation of more photogenerated electrons, which in turn generates a large amount of O2 –. In contrast, in PG-CNS-0.3, the significant decrease in exciton dissociation leads to a greater tendency to generate 1O2 through an energy transfer process (Eqn. (5)). At this point, the yield of T1 excitons would become the main factor affecting 1O2 production. For polymer semiconductors, T1 excitons arise primarily from S1 excitons through the ISC process [83]. Unluckily, it has been shown that the ISC process from S1 to T1 has an extremely large energy barrier, resulting in practically very low conversion efficiency with only a small amount of 1O2 production [7,16,84]. Therefore, it is speculated that the number of free radicals is the main reason for the performance differences between the two.

According to the results aforementioned, Scheme 1 presented a possible photocatalytic mechanism for PG-CNS-0.2. The pristine g-C3N4 can only be photoexcited at wavelengths below 450 nm due to the wide bandgap. Besides, because of strong Coulomb interactions, the e––h+ pairs generated by the photoexcitation of g-C3N4 easily combine and form hard-to-dissociate excitons, which severely inhibit the improvement of photocatalytic efficiency. However, the modified PG-CNS-0.2 sample exhibited significantly enhanced photocatalytic efficiency for the following reasons. (1) The enhancement of the range and efficiency for light utilization. The midgap state induced by P-doping can expand the optical absorption edge of g-C3N4 from 450 nm to 560 nm, allowing it to take full use of light obtained by N-GQDs upconversion (450–550 nm), thereby enabling PG-CNS-0.2 extends photoresponse range to NIR region (>780 nm). (2) The improvement of exciton dissociation and carrier separation efficiency. In contrast to CNS, the E b in PG-CNS-0.2 is significantly lower under the combined effect of P-doping and coupling N-GQDs, suggesting that more excitons will dissociate into free charges easier. Moreover, N-GQDs have excellent electron conduction ability, which can accelerate the transfer process of successfully dissociated electrons and thus speed up exciton dissociation. (3) Endowing PG-CNS-0.2 with 2D ultrathin porous structure can offer more active sites and closer transfer routes for free charges, thus facilitating efficient charge separation and migration. With the synergistic effect of the above factors, more ROS are available to participate in various redox reactions, resulting in a considerable improvement in photocatalytic efficiency. PG-CNS-0.2 is photoexcited to produce e––h+ pairs, a small amount of which would successfully dissociate into free electrons and holes (Eqn. (1)), while a substantial amount would bind to generate S1 excitons due to Coulomb interactions (Eqn. (2)). Under the synergistic effect of various modification methods, more S1 excitons speed up the dissociation into free electrons and holes (Eqn. (3)), which in turn participate in a series of redox reactions (Eqn. (6)–(12)). Meanwhile, with a very small probability, S1 excitons would convert to T1 excitons by the ISC process (Eqn. (4)), and the resulting T1 excitons would generate 1O2 by energy transfer process with O2 (Eqn. (5)). Notably, the CB (–0.60 eV vs NHE) and VB (2.11 eV vs NHE) of PG-CNS-0.2 can satisfy the O2/O2 – (–0.046 eV vs NHE), OH/OH– (1.99 eV vs NHE), and H2 (0 eV vs NHE) generation potentials [85,86]. Finally, the various ROS (h+, O2 –, 1O2, OH, and H2O2) generated by the above processes would together participate in the contaminant degradation (Eqn. (12)), while photoinduced e– would be utilized for H2 production (Eqn. (6)).

In conclusion, P-doping and coupling N-GQDs were jointly used to modify ultrathin porous g-C3N4 nanosheets to prepare a novel nonmetallic PG-CNS-0.2 composite photocatalysts in this study. DRS, ESR and photocatalytic performance tests confirm that the photoresponse range of PG-CNS-0.2 can be extended above 780 nm, enabling better utilization of solar energy. This results from the midgap state induced by P-doping and excellent upconversion performance of N-GQDs. In addition, the results of PL and electrochemical tests revealed that exciton dissociation and charge transfer ability within PG-CNS-0.2 were significantly enhanced. This is due to the reduced E b caused by P-doping, enhanced electron conduction caused by N-GQDs, and shorter migration path provided by the porous structure. Lastly, radical trapping tests revealed that ROS targeted regulation could be achieved by changing the doping amount of N-GQDs, which in turn could improve the controllability of photocatalytic reactions. The H2 production activity and TC degradation efficiency of PG-CNS-0.2 were dramatically improved owing to the optimized photoresponsiveness and exciton dissociation efficiency. This work not only reveals the exciton and carrier behaviors of carbon nitride-based composites in the photocatalytic process in detail but also introduces a novel perspective and strategy for designing broad-spectrum responsive photocatalysts.

Yao Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Jingshu Yuan: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft.
